title: Multi-Objective Model Selection for Time Series Forecasting

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - MULTI-OBJECTIVE MODEL SELECTION FOR TIME SE RIES FORECASTING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Research on time series forecasting has predominantly focused on developing methods that improve accuracy. However, other criteria such as training time or latency are critical in many real-world applications. We therefore address the question of how to choose an appropriate forecasting model for a given dataset among the plethora of available forecasting methods when accuracy is only one of many criteria. For this, our contributions are two-fold. First, we present a comprehensive benchmark, evaluating 7 classical and 6 deep learning forecasting methods on 44 heterogeneous, publicly available datasets. The benchmark code is open-sourced along with evaluations and forecasts for all methods. These evaluations enable us to answer open questions such as the amount of data required for deep learning models to outperform classical ones. Second, we leverage the benchmark evaluations to learn good defaults that consider multiple objectives such as accuracy and latency. By learning a mapping from forecasting models to performance metrics, we show that our method PARETOSELECT is able to accurately select models from the Pareto front — alleviating the need to train or evaluate many forecasting models for model selection. To the best of our knowledge, PARETOSELECT constitutes the first method to learn default models in a multi-objective setting.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

For decades, businesses have been using time series forecasting to drive strategic decision-making (Simchi-Levi et al., 2013; Hyndman & Athanasopoulos, 2018; Petropoulos et al., 2020). Analysts leverage forecasts to gauge resource requirements, retailers forecast future product demand to optimize their supply chains, cloud providers predict future web traffic to scale server fleets, and in the energy sector, forecasting plays a crucial role e.g. to predict load and energy prices. In domains like these and many other, more precise predictions directly translate into an increase in profit. Thus, it is no surprise that research on forecasting methods has historically focused on improving accuracy.

In addition to more classical _local_ forecasting methods which fit a model per time series, _global_ forecasting models such as deep learning and tree-based models have demonstrated state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy (Wen et al., 2017; Oreshkin et al., 2019; Salinas et al., 2020a; Smyl, 2020) when sufficient training data is available (Makridakis et al., 2018; Januschowski et al., 2020). This research has led to a large variety of different forecasting models and hyperparameter choices (Hutter et al., 2019), where different models exhibit vastly different characteristics, including accuracy, training time, model size, and inference latency.

While this variety of forecasting models is a great resource, it introduces challenging questions. On the one hand, researchers would like to understand patterns in the performance of different models and to benchmark new models against existing methods. On the other hand, practitioners are interested in understanding which model performs best for a particular dataset or application.

In this work, we address both of these problems: we release one of the most comprehensive publicly available evaluations of forecasting models across 44 datasets. Using this benchmark dataset, we develop a novel method for learning _good defaults_ for forecasting models on previously unseen datasets. Importantly, we adopt a multi-objective perspective that allows us to select forecasting models that are simultaneously accurate and satisfy constraints such as inference latency, training time, or model size.

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

The main contributions of this work can be summarized as follows:

- We release the evaluations of 13 forecasting methods on 44 public datasets with respect to multiple performance criteria (different forecast accuracy metrics, inference latency, training time, and model size). This constitutes, by far, the most comprehensive publicly available evaluation of forecasting methods. Those evaluations can be leveraged, for example, to assess the relative performance of future forecasting methods with little effort.

- As an example application of this benchmark, we use the data to perform a statistical analysis which shows that only a few thousands observations are required for deep learning methods to outperform classical methods. In addition, we investigate the benefit of ensembling forecasting models.
- We propose a novel method that can leverage offline evaluations to learn good default models for unseen datasets. Default models are selected to optimize for multiple objectives.

- We introduce a technique for ensembling models in a multi-objective setting where the resulting ensemble is not only highly accurate but also optimized for other objectives, such as a low inference latency.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Time Series Forecasting.** Time series forecasting has seen a recent surge in attention by the academic community that has started to reflect its relevance in business applications. Traditionally, univariate, so-called _local_ models that consider time series individually have dominated (Hyndman & Athanasopoulos, 2018). However, in modern applications, methods that learn _globally_ across a set of time series can be more accurate (Oreshkin et al., 2019; Salinas et al., 2020a; Lim et al., 2021; Montero-Manso & Hyndman, 2021) — in particular, methods that rely on deep learning. With a considerable choice of models available, it is unclear which methods should perform best on which forecasting dataset, unlike in other machine learning domains where dominant approaches exist.

In domains such as computer vision or neural architecture search (NAS), offline computations have been harvested and leveraged successfully to perform extensive model comparisons or compare different NAS strategies (Ying et al., 2019; Dong & Yang, 2020; Pfisterer et al., 2021). However, to the best of our knowledge, no comprehensive set of model evaluations has been released in the realm of forecasting. Consequently, some questions remain open: how much data is required for global deep learning methods to outperform classical local methods such as ARIMA or ETS (Hyndman & Athanasopoulos, 2018)? What is the impact on accuracy when ensembling different forecasting models? While some recent methods incorporate ensembling (Oreshkin et al., 2019; Jeon & Seong, 2021), comparisons are often made against individual models which may cloud the benefit of the method proposed versus the sole benefit of ensembling.

**Learning Default Models.** Finding the best model or set of hyperparameters is often performed via Bayesian optimization given its theoretical regret guarantees (Srinivas et al., 2012). However, even with early-stopping techniques (Golovin et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017), practitioners often restrict the search to a single model due to the large cost of training many models. One technique to drastically speed up the model/hyperparameter search is to reuse offline evaluations of related datasets via transfer learning. For instance, Wistuba et al. (2015); Winkelmolen et al. (2020); Pfisterer et al. (2021) leverage offline evaluations to alleviate the need for training many models by learning a small list of _n_ good defaults that provide a small joint error when evaluated on all datasets. This approach bears a lot of similarity with ours. Key differences are that we consider multiple objectives and model ensembles. In the time series domain, Shah et al. (2021) also considers the task of automatically choosing from a large pool of forecasting models the one performing best on a particular dataset but also only optimizes for a single objective and relies on potentially expensive model training for model selection.

## 3 BENCHMARKING FORECASTING METHODS

In this section, we formally introduce the problem of time series forecasting and subsequently provide an overview of the benchmark evaluations that we release. By evaluating 13 forecasting meth-

## 3.1 TIME SERIES FORECASTING

The goal of time series forecasting is to predict the future values of one or more time series based on historical observations. In probabilistic time series forecasting, a model then estimates the probability distribution across the forecast horizon _τ_ , i.e. the distribution over the _τ_ future values, from the historical observations:

## 3.2 METHODS

We consider 13 models in total that can be distinguished into 7 _local_ methods (estimating parameters individually from each time series) and 6 _global_ methods (learning from all available time series jointly). Details about models can be found in Appendix A.

**Local Methods.** We use _Seasonal Naïve_ (Hyndman & Athanasopoulos, 2018) and _NPTS_ (Rangapuram et al., 2021) as simple, non-parametric baselines. Further, we consider _ARIMA_ and _ETS_ (Hyndman & Athanasopoulos, 2018) as well-known statistical methods and _STL-AR_ (Talagala, 2021) and _Theta_ (Assimakopoulos & Nikolopoulos, 2000) as inexpensive alternatives. Lastly, we include _Prophet_ (Taylor & Letham, 2018), an interpretable model that has received plenty of attention.

**Global Methods.** All global methods that we use are deep learning models, namely: _Simple Feedforward_ (Alexandrov et al., 2020), _MQ-CNN_ and _MQ-RNN_ (Wen et al., 2017), _DeepAR_ (Salinas et al., 2020a), _N-BEATS_ (Oreshkin et al., 2019), and _TFT_ (Lim et al., 2021). For each model (except MQ-RNN), we consider three hyperparameter settings: this includes the default set provided by their implementation as well as hyperparameter sets that roughly halve and double the default model capacity (see Table 2 in the appendix). Additionally, we consider three different context lengths that govern the length of the time series that predictions are conditioned on.

**Model Training.** Model training is only required for deep learning models since parametric local methods estimate parameters at prediction time. Deep learning models are trained for a fixed duration depending on the size of the dataset. Further details can be found in Appendix C.

## 3.3 DATASETS

Our benchmark provides 44 heterogeneous public datasets in total. The datasets greatly differ in the number of time series (from 8 to _≈_ 170,000), their mean length (from _≈_ 22 to _≈_ 500,000), their frequency (minutely, hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, yearly), and the forecast horizon (from 6 to 60). Thus, we expect them to cover a wide range of datasets encountered in practice.

Datasets are taken from various forecasting competitions, the UCI (Dua & Graff, 2017), and the Monash time series forecasting repository (Godahewa et al., 2021). Dataset sources, descriptions, basic statistics, and an explanation of the data preparation procedure can be found in Appendix B.

## 3.4 METRICS

To measure the accuracy of forecasting models, we employ the normalized _continuous ranked probability score_ (nCRPS) (Matheson & Winkler, 1976; Gneiting & Raftery, 2007) whose definition

## 3.5 BENCHMARK ANALYSIS

Having outlined the benchmark, we now want to demonstrate the usefulness of the evaluations for research. For this, we want to analyze how local (classical) and global (deep learning) forecasting methods compare against each other. In fact, this comparison has been the object of heated discussions in the forecasting community (Makridakis et al., 2018; Januschowski et al., 2020).

**Method Comparison.** In order to compare methods, we consider their latency and accuracy (in terms of nCRPS) across all datasets. Table 1 shows each method’s relative latency compared to Seasonal Naïve as well as the the methods’ nCRPS rank[1] . As far as latency is concerned, global methods, once trained, allow to generate forecasts considerably faster than local methods (except for Seasonal Naïve). The reason is simple: once trained, they simply need to run a forward pass. In contrast, the implementations for the local methods chosen here do not differentiate between training and inference, but rather estimate their parameters at prediction time. Across datasets, the relative latency of all considered deep learning models improves between 15% and 95% upon the latency of the fastest local method (excluding Seasonal Naïve). As far as accuracy is concerned, complex deep learning models — namely DeepAR and TFT — generally perform best across the benchmark datasets. Except for MQ-RNN, global methods compare favorably against local methods.

As a measure of _relative_ model stability, Table 1 additionally shows the standard deviation of the nCRPS rank across all benchmark datasets. Not only is TFT the method which performs best on average, its rank is also comparatively consistent compared to other methods. Especially,

Table 1 also indicates the performance of ensembles obtained by averaging the predictions of _n_ = 9 ( _n_ = 3 for MQ-RNN) deep learning models trained with different hyperparameters ( _hyperensembles_ ) as done for N-BEATS (Oreshkin et al., 2019) or in M5 competition’s third-ranking method (Jeon & Seong, 2021). While generally bearing a significant cost in latency, the benefit of ensembling is significant. For instance, the Simple Feedforward hyper-ensemble yields a competitive model that outperforms DeepAR in terms of both accuracy and latency. Lastly, DeepAR and TFT hyper-ensembles result in the most competitive ensembles.

**Comparison of Classical and Deep Learning Methods.** We further conduct a statistical analysis to compare the classical time series models with the deep learning models listed in Section 3.2. For this, we construct the null hypothesis that the accuracy of classical models is equal to or better than the accuracy of deep learning methods. This is a claim put forward, for example, by Makridakis et al. (2018). More formally, we write _H_ 0 : _Q_ class _≤Q_ deep where _Q_ class and _Q_ deep describe the distribution of nCRPS values of classical and deep learning models, respectively. Samples of the distributions are derived from the respective single-model evaluations in our benchmark. _Q_ class and _Q_ deep are continuous and belong to unknown families of distributions with unequal variances. Hence, we choose the nonparametric two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (Gibbons & Chakraborti, 2003) to compute the _p_ -value, i.e. the probability of _H_ 0 holding true, on each individual dataset.

We evaluate the null hypothesis _H_ 0 on all 44 benchmark datasets and plot the _p_ -value for the different dataset sizes in Figure 1. At a significance level of _α_ = 5%, _H_ 0 can be rejected for 30 out of 44 datasets. In fact, deep learning models exhibit competitive performance for almost all datasets and regularly outperform classical methods. Further, they tend to perform comparatively better with increasing dataset size — even if for some large datasets, classical models are indistinguishable — and, nonetheless, show competitive performance for small datasets. The latter is of particular interest since it contradicts tribal knowledge that large datasets are needed to outperform local/classical methods and disputes the claim presented by Makridakis et al. (2018) since only a few thousands observations seem sufficient to outperform the classical models considered.

These statements are further supported by Figure 2 where we compare the best deep learning model _x_ deep against the best classical model _x_ class on each dataset. We compute the relative improvement given by deep learning models as nCRPS[nCRPS] ([(] _x[x]_ class[dee][p][)] ) _[−]_[1][.][Except][for][few][outliers,][the][best][deep][learning] model outperforms all classical models on 40 out of 44 datasets (see Appendix F for a further analysis). Fitting a linear regression model on the relative improvements shows that the improvement by using deep learning models is only slightly amplified as the datasets grow in size.

We note, that we conflate deep learning and global models in our above discussion. MonteroManso & Hyndman (2021) show the general superiority of global models over local models both theoretically and empirically and our results confirm their findings further.

## 4 LEARNING MULTI-OBJECTIVE DEFAULTS

The benchmark introduced in the previous section outlined how to acquire offline evaluations of forecasting methods on various datasets. However, in the presence of multiple conflicting objectives

such as accuracy and latency, it is not clear how one could choose the best models. In this section, we first formalize the problem of learning good defaults from these evaluations for a single objective. Afterwards, we formally introduce multi-objective optimization and show how learning defaults can be extended to account for multiple objectives. In the end, we report experimental results obtained by using our method.

## 4.1 PREREQUISITES

In our setting, we consider an objective function _**f**_ : _X →_ R _[m]_ for a given task (in our case, a task is a dataset). The objective function maps any time series model _x ∈X_ to _m_ objectives (such as nCRPS, latency, etc.) that ought to be minimized. In addition, we assume that model evaluations on _T_ different but related tasks with objective functions _**f**_[(1)] _, . . . ,_ _**f**_[(] _[T]_[ )] are available. The set of offline model evaluations is then given as
where _Nj_ evaluations are available for task _j_ and _**y** i_[(] _[j]_[)] denotes the evaluation of _x_[(] _i[j]_[)] on task _j_ .

**Learning Defaults.** To learn a set of _n_ good default models for unseen datasets in the singleobjective setting, Pfisterer et al. (2021) propose to pick the models that minimize the joint error obtained when evaluating them on all datasets. This amount to pick the set of models _{x_ 1 _, . . . , xn} ⊂ X_ which minimize the following objective:

where _k_ denotes a single fixed objective (for instance, the classification error). Since the problem is NP-complete, a greedy heuristic with a provable approximation guarantee is used: it iteratively adds the model minimizing Eq. 3 to the current selection. However, this approach only works for a single objective of choice _fk_ rather than taking all objectives _**f**_ (such as accuracy and latency) into account. In addition, it does not easily support the selection of ensembles since objectives can interact in differently when models are ensembled: while accuracy will likely increase by a small amount, the latencies of ensemble members need to be added up. Taking into account all possible ensembles of a given size _n_ would also blow up the combinatoric search when optimizing Eq. 3.

**Multi-Objective Optimization.** In multi-objective optimization, there generally exists no singular solution. Thus, when considering the objective function _**f**_ , there exists no single optimal model, but rather a set of optimal models (Emmerich & Deutz, 2018). A model _x ∈X_ dominates another model _x[′] ∈X_ ( _x ≺_ _**f** x[′]_ ) if _fi_ ( _x_ ) _≤ fi_ ( _x[′]_ ) for all _i_ and there exists some _i_ such that _fi_ ( _x_ ) _< fi_ ( _x[′]_ ). The set of all non-dominated solutions (i.e. models) is denoted as the Pareto front _P_ _**f**_ ( _X_ ) whose members are commonly referred to as Pareto-optimal solutions:

To quantify the quality of a set _S_ = _{x_ 1 _, . . . , xn} ⊂X_ of selected models, we use the hypervolume error (Zitzler & Thiele, 1998; Li & Yao, 2019). Given a set of points _Y ⊂_ R _[m]_ and a reference point _**r** ∈_ R _[m]_ , we first define the hypervolume as the Lebesgue measure Λ of the dominated space:
In turn, the hypervolume error _ε_ of the set _S_ is defined as the difference in hypervolume compared to the true Pareto front _P_ _**f**_ ( _X_ ):

Thus, the hypervolume error _ε_ ( _S_ ) reaches its minimum when _S ⊇P_ _**f**_ ( _X_ ). To account for different scales in the optimization objectives, we further standardize all objectives by quantile normalization (Bolstad et al., 2003) such that they follow a uniform distribution _U_ (0 _,_ 1) — this is feasible as _X_ is finite. While the normalization makes our comparisons robust to monotonic transformations of the objectives (Binois et al., 2020), it also allows us to choose the reference point for Eq. 5 as _**r**_ = **1** _m_ .

**Learning Multi-Objective Defaults.** Using the hypervolume error, we can extend the minimization problem of Eq. 3 to learn defaults in the multi-objective setting. For this, we propose the following minimization problem:

In words, we seek to find a set of complementary model configurations _{x_ 1 _, . . . , xn} ⊂X_ that provide a good approximation of the Pareto front _P_ _**f**_ ( _j_ ) ( _X_ ) across tasks _j ∈{_ 1 _, . . . , T }_ .

## 4.2 PARETOSELECT

We now introduce PARETOSELECT which tackles the minimization of Eq. 7. On a high-level, PARETOSELECT works as follows: first, it fits a parametric surrogate model _**f**_[˜] _θ_ that predicts the _**f**_ performances of model configurations by leveraging the evaluations of˜ _θ_ to estimate the objectives for all models in _X_ and applies the non-dominated sorting algorithm _D_ . Then, it uses the surrogate on the objectives to select a list of default models.

The surrogate model _**f**_[˜] _θ_ is trained by attempting to correctly rank model configurations within each task in the available offline evaluations _D_ . For each model _x ∈X_ , _**f**_[˜] _θ_ outputs a vector _y_ ˜ _∈_ R _[m]_ for _m_ optimization objectives. Models can then be ranked for each objective independently. In order to fit _**f**_[˜] _θ_ on the data _D_ , we use listwise ranking (Xia et al., 2008) and apply linear discounting to focus on correctly identifying the top configurations similar to Zügner et al. (2020). The corresponding loss function can be found in Appendix D.

For the parametric surrogate model _**f**_[˜] _θ_ , we choose a simple MLP similar to Salinas et al. (2020b). We use two hidden layers of size 32 with LeakyReLU activations after each hidden layer and apply a weight decay of 0.01 as regularization. Importantly, we do not tune these hyperparameters. Predictive performances for every model can eventually be computed as

We note that for minimizing Eq. 7, either regression or ranking objectives can be used: since we apply quantile normalization to compute the hypervolume, the magnitude of the values predicted by _**f**_ ˜ _θ_ is irrelevant. However, we find the ranking setting to be superior to regression for finding good default models (see Appendix D for more details).

**Multi-Objective Sorting.** The “best” configurations are then determined by applying the nondominated sorting algorithm (NDSA) — an extension of sorting to the multi-dimensional setting (Srinivas & Deb, 1994; Emmerich & Deutz, 2018) — to the predictive performances _Y_[˜] . Figure 3 provides an illustration of the sorting procedure. NDSA can be described as a two-level procedure. First, it partitions the configurations to be sorted in multiple layers by iteratively computing the Pareto fronts, then it applies a sorting function sort to rank the configurations in each layer:

We choose sort to compute an _ϵ_ -net (Clarkson, 2006) for which there exists a simple greedy algorithm. When sorting a set _X_ , the _ϵ_ -net sorting operation yields an order sort( _X_ ) = [ _x_ 1 _, . . . , x|X|_ ]. The first element _x_ 1 is chosen randomly from _X_ and _xi_ +1 is defined iteratively as the item which is farthest from the current selection in terms of Euclidean distance, formally:

The final ordering is eventually obtained by concatenating the sorted layers. While previous work followed different approaches for the sort operation in the NDSA algorithm (Srinivas & Deb, 1994; Deb et al., 2002), we leverage the _ϵ_ -net since Salinas et al. (2021) highlighted its theoretical guarantees and Schmucker et al. (2021) provided empirical evidence for its good performance.

The pseudo-code of PARETOSELECT is given in Algorithm 1 in the appendix. In the case where predictions of the surrogate are error-free, the recommendations of the algorithm can be guaranteed to be perfect with zero hypervolume error.

## 4.3 RESULTS

In order to evaluate our approach, we leverage the data collected with the benchmark presented in Section 3. For evaluation, we perform leave-one-out-cross-validation where we use each dataset one after _**f**_ ˜ _θ_ on the remainingthe other as the 43test datasets.dataset andThe multi-objective setting in the following experiments aims toestimate the parameters _θ_ of our parametric surrogate model minimize latency as well as nCRPS.

In Figure 4, we report the average hypervolume obtained for PARETOSELECT and several baselines, averaged over all 44 test datasets. PARETOSELECT picks the top elements of the non-dominated sort on the surrogate’s predictive performances. As baselines, we consider a variant of PARETOSELECT which considers the nCRPS as the single objective ( **Single-Objective** ), the approach of Pfisterer et al. (2021) which greedily picks models to minimize the joint nCRPS on the training data ( **Greedy Model-Free** ), and random search over the entire model space of size _|X|_ = 247 ( **Random** ). The hypervolume obtained by PARETOSELECT with 10 default models is small ( _≈_ 0 _._ 06) compared to the best baseline that reaches _≈_ 0 _._ 20. Further, random search requires a total of 27 models to be on par with the hypervolume of only 10 models selected via our method.

Figure 5 depicts the top _n_ = 10 recommendations produced by PARETOSELECT when predicting default models on a single dataset. The default models cover the true Pareto front well: our method is able to pick models with low latency, low nCRPS, and a good trade-off between these two objectives.

## 4.4 MULTI-OBJECTIVE ENSEMBLING

As shown in Section 3.5, ensembling models yields significant gains in accuracy but comes at the cost of considerable latency. Since a massively large ensemble model is of no practical use, it is critical to be able to select an ensemble with a latency that is acceptable for an application. Thus, we consider ensembles under different latency constraints.

For a latency constraint _c_ , we build an ensemble with _n ≤_ 10 members _x_ 1 _, . . . , xn_ by iteratively picking the model with best nCRPS such that the ensemble latency constrain _c_ is still met. To generate ensemble predictions, we combine the forecasts of the base models _x_ 1 _, . . . , xn_ by averaging all 10-quantiles _{_ 0 _._ 1 _, . . . ,_ 0 _._ 9 _}_ for each time series and time step independently. Albeit the performances of the base models might differ significantly, our experiments showed that more complicated, non-uniform weighting schemes yielded less accurate forecasts on average. This aligns with current literature showing that simple averaging is often most effective (Petropoulos et al., 2020).

Figure 6 shows the performance of different latency-constrained ensembles, relative to the performance of an unconstrained ensemble that picks the _n_ = 10 models with lowest predicted nCRPS. As expected, higher latency constraints _c_ allows the ensemble to perform better. For _c ≥_ 100 ms, the constrained ensemble recovers the Pareto front and outperforms all individual models _x ∈X_ as well as ensembles. The small gap to the Pareto front for constraints _c ≤_ 50 ms can be attributed to multiple effects. First, the nCRPS values predicted by the surrogate _**f**_[˜] _θ_ are not optimal. Second, models may not satisfy _c_ on every dataset although they have a lower latency on average (e.g. the default TFT configuration violates _c_ = 2.5 ms on 17/44 datasets). Table 1 additionally reports performance metrics of latency-constrained ensembles: for instance, the ensemble for _c_ = 10 ms outperforms all individual base models in terms of average nCRPS rank while the ensemble for _c_ = 100 ms outperforms all hyper-ensembles as well.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this work, we have presented (i) a new benchmark for evaluating forecasting methods in terms of multiple metrics and (ii) PARETOSELECT, a new algorithm that can learn default model configurations in a multi-objective setting. In future work, we will consider applying those techniques to other domains such as computer vision where offline evaluations of multiple datasets were recently made available by Dong & Yang (2020). Additionally, future research may extend the presented predictive surrogate model to the case of ensembles: this would enable PARETOSELECT to be used directly to select members for ensembles of any latency.

9

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 6 REPRODUCIBILITY

Public datasets and method implementations were used to ensure the reproducibility of our experiments. In Appendix A, we provide all hyperparameters used for training the benchmark forecasting models via GluonTS. The source and processing of each dataset as well as the procedure for generating data splits is detailed in Appendix B. We also provide details on the surrogate model used and a comparison with other choices in Appendix D. The pseudocode of PARETOSELECT is given in Appendix E. As a source of truth, we share the entire code used to run the benchmark and its evaluation with the submission. This code as well as the generated evaluations will be released with the paper.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a comprehensive benchmark for multi-objective time series forecasting and proposes ParetoSelect, a method for learning default model configurations. While the benchmark is valuable and the method is novel, the paper suffers from several critical issues: insufficient statistical validation of benchmark results, inadequate evaluation of ParetoSelect's generalization, unclear assumptions about objective importance, and potential scalability concerns.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by releasing a large-scale benchmark of forecasting methods across multiple objectives, which addresses a clear gap in the field. The ParetoSelect method for multi-objective model selection is novel and theoretically sound. However, the experimental evaluation has significant weaknesses, particularly in statistical validation and generalization testing, which undermine confidence in the claimed results.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient Statistical Validation of Benchmark Results: The paper's key claim about deep learning models outperforming classical methods with only a few thousand observations relies on a single Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (Figure 1). This test compares distributions without accounting for multiple comparisons across datasets or objectives. The paper lacks proper statistical significance testing for the multi-objective trade-offs reported in Table 1 and Figure 6. Without correction for multiple hypothesis testing or confidence intervals on the hypervolume metrics, readers cannot assess the reliability of these performance differences. This is particularly problematic given the high variance reported (e.g., nCRPS rank standard deviations of 3-7 in Table 1). The authors should include statistical significance tests with appropriate corrections (e.g., Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg) for all pairwise comparisons and report confidence intervals on key metrics.

- Unclear Generalization of ParetoSelect to Unseen Datasets: The leave-one-dataset-out evaluation of ParetoSelect (Section 4.3) is insufficient to demonstrate generalization. The 44 datasets, while heterogeneous, may share underlying patterns that the surrogate model exploits. The paper provides no analysis of how ParetoSelect performs on truly novel dataset characteristics (e.g., different frequencies, seasonal patterns, or data distributions) not represented in the benchmark. Figure 4 shows average hypervolume error but does not break down performance by dataset characteristics or identify failure cases. The authors should include an analysis of ParetoSelect's performance across dataset types and demonstrate its robustness to out-of-distribution datasets, perhaps through synthetic data generation or by holding out entire dataset families.

- Unjustified Assumption of Equal Objective Importance: The paper treats accuracy (nCRPS) and latency as equally important objectives without justification. In practice, these objectives have vastly different business implications depending on the application. The hypervolume metric (Eq. 6) implicitly assumes equal weighting after quantile normalization, but this normalization may not reflect real-world preferences. The latency-constrained ensembles (Section 4.4) hint at varying priorities but do not provide a framework for incorporating user-specified preferences. The authors should discuss how different objective weightings affect the Pareto front and provide guidance on setting these weights for different application scenarios. They could also incorporate user preference learning into ParetoSelect.

- Limited Scalability Analysis for Large-Scale Applications: The paper claims ParetoSelect alleviates the need to train many models but provides no analysis of its computational requirements for large-scale model spaces. The current model space has only 247 configurations (Section 4.3), which is modest compared to real-world hyperparameter search spaces that can include thousands of configurations. The surrogate model training time and prediction latency are not reported, nor is the method's performance as the model space grows. The authors should evaluate ParetoSelect's scalability with respect to the number of models, hyperparameters, and objectives, and compare its computational overhead against the cost of training additional models. This is crucial for practical adoption in large-scale forecasting systems.

- Inadequate Ablation Study of ParetoSelect Components: The paper compares ParetoSelect against several baselines but does not systematically ablate its key components: the surrogate model architecture, the ranking loss function, and the non-dominated sorting algorithm. Appendix D mentions regression vs. ranking but provides no detailed comparison. The choice of MLP architecture (two layers of size 32) is presented without justification or sensitivity analysis. The epsilon-net sorting (Eq. 10) is compared only implicitly through overall performance. The authors should include ablation studies showing how each component contributes to performance and provide sensitivity analysis for key hyperparameters of ParetoSelect itself.

- Missing demonstration of ParetoSelect on constrained ensembles: The paper introduces latency-constrained ensembles in Section 4.4 but never demonstrates how ParetoSelect could be extended to select these ensembles directly. The current approach uses a simple greedy selection under latency constraints, which is separate from ParetoSelect's core algorithm. Since the paper claims ParetoSelect constitutes the first method for learning defaults in multi-objective settings, it should show how the method handles ensemble selection natively. The authors should extend ParetoSelect to predict ensemble performance directly (as mentioned in the conclusion) and demonstrate this on at least one dataset, comparing the results against the current greedy approach. This would complete the narrative about multi-objective selection for both individual models and ensembles.

- No worked example of surrogate model predictions: While ParetoSelect uses a surrogate model to predict performance, the paper never shows a concrete example of these predictions for a specific dataset and model configuration. Readers cannot verify how the surrogate model handles different model families or hyperparameters. The authors should select one dataset (e.g., M4 Yearly) and show the surrogate's predicted vs. actual nCRPS and latency for a few representative models (e.g., TFT default, Simple Feedforward small capacity, Seasonal Naïve). This would help readers understand the surrogate's accuracy and how errors in predictions might affect the Pareto front approximation.

- Unexplored computational efficiency of ParetoSelect: The paper claims ParetoSelect alleviates the need to train many models but provides no quantitative analysis of its computational overhead. The surrogate model training time, prediction latency, and memory usage are not reported, nor is there a comparison against the cost of evaluating additional models. For practical adoption, readers need to know whether ParetoSelect's overhead is justified by the reduction in model evaluations. The authors should measure and report the time required for surrogate training and prediction, and compare this against the time saved by reducing the number of models that need to be evaluated. This analysis should include scaling behavior with different numbers of models and objectives.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive statistical significance testing with multiple comparison corrections for all benchmark results

- Include detailed analysis of ParetoSelect's generalization across dataset characteristics and out-of-distribution scenarios

- Provide framework and analysis for incorporating unequal objective weights based on application requirements

- Conduct scalability experiments evaluating ParetoSelect's performance with larger model spaces and computational overhead analysis

- Add systematic ablation study of ParetoSelect's components and sensitivity analysis of its hyperparameters

comments.title: Incorrect relative improvement formula
comments.quote: We compute the relative improvement given by deep learning models as  $\frac{n\mathrm{CRPS}(x_{\mathrm{deep}})}{n\mathrm{CRPS}(x_{\mathrm{class}})} - 1$ .
comments.explanation: The formula for relative improvement is incorrect. As written, this computes the relative difference, not improvement. Since lower nCRPS values indicate better performance, the correct formula for relative improvement should be $\frac{n\mathrm{CRPS}(x_{\mathrm{class}}) - n\mathrm{CRPS}(x_{\mathrm{deep}})}{n\mathrm{CRPS}(x_{\mathrm{class}})} = 1 - \frac{n\mathrm{CRPS}(x_{\mathrm{deep}})}{n\mathrm{CRPS}(x_{\mathrm{class}})}$. With the current formula, a negative value would indicate improvement, which is counterintuitive for "improvement" metrics. Rewrite the formula as $1 - \frac{n\mathrm{CRPS}(x_{\mathrm{deep}})}{n\mathrm{CRPS}(x_{\mathrm{class}})}$ because this yields positive values when deep learning models improve accuracy.

comments.title: Proof incorrectly assumes first k recommendations equal Pareto front
comments.quote: Proof. By Eq. 9, the first  $k$  recommendations of Pareto Select are such that  $\{x_1,\ldots ,x_k\} = \mathcal{P}_{\hat{f}_\theta}(\mathcal{X})$  where  $k = |\mathcal{P}_{\hat{f}_\theta}(\mathcal{X})|$ . Since for all  $x\in \mathcal{X}$ ,  $\hat{f}_{\theta}(x) = f(x)$ , we get that  $\mathcal{P}_{\hat{f}_\theta}(\mathcal{X}) = \mathcal{P}_f(\mathcal{X})$ . It follows that for any  $k\leq n$ ,  $\mathcal{P}_f(\mathcal{X}) = \{x_1,\dots,x_k\} \subset \{x_1,\dots,x_n\}$  and consequently  $\varepsilon (\{x_1,\ldots ,x_n\}) = 0$  since the hypervolume error of any set of points containing the Pareto front is zero.
comments.explanation: The proof incorrectly claims that the first k recommendations equal the Pareto front. Equation 9 shows that X₁ = sort(P_{\hat{f}_θ}(X)), meaning the first layer contains the Pareto front but the sort operation reorders these points. The ε-net sorting (Eq. 10) selects points based on maximizing Euclidean distance, which can intermix Pareto front points with others. Therefore, the claim that {x₁,...,xₖ} = P_{\hat{f}_θ}(X) is false. Rewrite the proof to correctly handle the sorting operation: 'By Eq. 9, the first layer X₁ contains the Pareto front P_{\hat{f}_θ}(X). Since n ≥ |P_f(X)| and the algorithm concatenates layers in order, the first n recommendations include all points from X₁ = P_{\hat{f}_θ}(X) = P_f(X). Therefore ε({x₁,...,xₙ}) = 0.'

comments.title: Contradictory claim about deep learning data requirements
comments.quote: As an example application of this benchmark, we use the data to perform a statistical analysis which shows that only a few thousands observations are required for deep learning methods to outperform classical methods.
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts the paper's own statistical analysis in Section 3.5. The hypothesis test results show that deep learning models outperform classical methods on 30 out of 44 datasets, but the paper doesn't establish a specific threshold of "a few thousand observations" as the tipping point. The analysis in Section 3.5 examines performance across dataset sizes but doesn't provide this quantitative threshold. The introduction should either remove this specific quantitative claim or reference the exact analysis that supports it. Rewrite as "we use the data to perform a statistical analysis which shows that deep learning methods can outperform classical methods even on relatively small datasets" to avoid making an unsupported quantitative claim.

comments.title: Contradictory statistical test interpretation
comments.quote: At a significance level of  $\alpha = 5\%$ ,  $H_0$  can be rejected for 30 out of 44 datasets. In fact, deep learning models exhibit competitive performance for almost all datasets and regularly outperform classical methods.
comments.explanation: There's a logical inconsistency in interpreting the statistical test results. The null hypothesis $H_0: \mathcal{Q}_{\mathrm{class}} \leq \mathcal{Q}_{\mathrm{deep}}$ states that classical models perform equal to or better than deep learning models. Rejecting $H_0$ for 30 out of 44 datasets means deep learning models outperform classical models on those 30 datasets. However, the text then claims "competitive performance for almost all datasets" which contradicts the fact that $H_0$ could NOT be rejected for 14 datasets (32% of the total). On these 14 datasets, there's insufficient evidence to claim deep learning superiority. The statement should be revised to acknowledge that deep learning models show statistically significant improvement on 30 datasets while the results are inconclusive for the remaining 14.

comments.title: Inconsistent hyperparameter configuration description
comments.quote: For each model (except MQ-RNN), we consider three hyperparameter settings: this includes the default set provided by their implementation as well as hyperparameter sets that roughly halve and double the default model capacity (see Table 2 in the appendix). Additionally, we consider three different context lengths that govern the length of the time series that predictions are conditioned on.
comments.explanation: The text describes two different hyperparameter variations (capacity and context length) but doesn't clarify whether these are combined multiplicatively or evaluated separately. If combined multiplicatively, this would create 3 × 3 = 9 configurations per model, which contradicts the earlier statement about total model count. The description needs to specify whether context length variations are applied to all capacity settings or only to the default configuration, as this affects the total number of model configurations being evaluated.

comments.title: Missing MQ-RNN hyperparameter justification
comments.quote: For each model (except MQ-RNN), we consider three hyperparameter settings: this includes the default set provided by their implementation as well as hyperparameter sets that roughly halve and double the default model capacity (see Table 2 in the appendix).
comments.explanation: The paper excludes MQ-RNN from the systematic hyperparameter variation without providing any justification. This creates an inconsistency in the experimental design where all other global models are evaluated across multiple capacity settings while MQ-RNN is only evaluated at its default configuration. The authors should either explain why MQ-RNN was excluded from this systematic variation or apply the same hyperparameter exploration to maintain experimental consistency.

comments.title: Incorrect objective function formulation
comments.quote: $\min_{\{x_{1},\dots,x_{n}\}\subset\mathcal{X}}\sum_{j=1}^{T}\ \min_{1\leq i\leq n}f_{k}^{(j)}(x_{i})$ (3)
comments.explanation: The objective function in Equation 3 is incorrectly formulated for the described problem. The text states the goal is to minimize "joint error obtained when evaluating them on all datasets," which should be the sum of the best model's performance on each dataset. However, the current formulation uses $\min_{1\leq i\leq n}f_{k}^{(j)}(x_{i})$ which selects the best performing model for each dataset independently, but doesn't account for the fact that the same set of $n$ models must be used across all datasets. The correct formulation should be $\min_{\{x_{1},\dots,x_{n}\}\subset\mathcal{X}}\sum_{j=1}^{T} \min_{1\leq i\leq n} f_{k}^{(j)}(x_{i})$ where the minimization over the model set is outside the sum over datasets. Rewrite Equation 3 as $\min_{\{x_{1},\dots,x_{n}\}\subset\mathcal{X}} \sum_{j=1}^{T} \min_{1\leq i\leq n} f_{k}^{(j)}(x_{i})$ because the current formulation incorrectly allows different model selections for different datasets.

comments.title: Inconsistent latency constraint description
comments.quote: For a latency constraint  $c$ , we build an ensemble with  $n \leq 10$  members  $x_{1},\ldots ,x_{n}$  by iteratively picking the model with best nCRPS such that the ensemble latency constrain  $c$  is still met.
comments.explanation: The text describes building ensembles under latency constraints but doesn't specify how the ensemble latency is calculated. Is it the sum of individual model latencies, the maximum, or some other aggregation? This ambiguity makes it impossible to verify whether the greedy selection algorithm actually satisfies the constraint. The authors should explicitly define the ensemble latency aggregation function before describing the selection procedure.

comments.title: Inconsistent hypervolume comparison claim
comments.quote: The hypervolume obtained by PARETOSELECT with 10 default models is small ( $\approx 0.06$ ) compared to the best baseline that reaches  $\approx 0.20$ . Further, random search requires a total of 27 models to be on par with the hypervolume of only 10 models selected via our method.
comments.explanation: There's a logical inconsistency in the hypervolume comparison. The text states PARETOSELECT achieves ≈0.06 hypervolume with 10 models, while the best baseline reaches ≈0.20. Then it claims random search needs 27 models to match PARETOSELECT's hypervolume. This doesn't make sense - if PARETOSELECT only achieves 0.06 and the best baseline achieves 0.20, why would random search need more models to match PARETOSELECT's lower performance? The claim should either state that random search needs 27 models to match the best baseline's 0.20 hypervolume, or clarify that PARETOSELECT's 0.06 is somehow superior despite being numerically smaller. Rewrite the passage as: "The hypervolume obtained by PARETOSELECT with 10 default models is ≈0.06. Further, random search requires a total of 27 models to achieve this same hypervolume, while the best baseline reaches ≈0.20 hypervolume."

comments.title: Missing reference point for hypervolume calculation
comments.quote: In Figure 4, we report the average hypervolume obtained for PARETOSELECT and several baselines, averaged over all 44 test datasets.
comments.explanation: The hypervolume metric requires specifying a reference point, but this critical detail is missing. Without knowing the reference point used for the hypervolume calculation, the reported values (≈0.06 and ≈0.20) are not interpretable. Different reference points would yield different hypervolume values, making comparisons meaningless. Add the reference point specification after this sentence: "The hypervolume is calculated using reference point (1.0, 1.0) for normalized latency and nCRPS values."

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about Pareto front recovery
comments.quote: For  $c \geq 100$  ms, the constrained ensemble recovers the Pareto front and outperforms all individual models  $x \in \mathcal{X}$  as well as ensembles.
comments.explanation: The claim that constrained ensembles 'recover the Pareto front' for $c \geq 100$ ms is not supported by the provided text. The Pareto front represents the optimal trade-off between accuracy and latency across all possible model configurations, but the text only describes a specific greedy selection method under latency constraints. Without showing that these ensembles actually lie on or approximate the Pareto front, this claim appears overstated. The authors should either provide evidence of Pareto optimality or soften this claim to state that the ensembles achieve 'near-optimal' performance.

comments.title: Overstated novelty of multi-objective approach
comments.quote: Key differences are that we consider multiple objectives and model ensembles. In the time series domain, *Shah et al. (2021)* also considers the task of automatically choosing from a large pool of forecasting models the one performing best on a particular dataset but also only optimizes for a single objective and relies on potentially expensive model training for model selection.
comments.explanation: The claim that considering multiple objectives is a key difference from prior work appears to overstate the novelty. According to the literature context, AutoXPCR (2023) and X-PCR (2024) already propose multi-objective model selection frameworks for time series forecasting that estimate predictive error, complexity, and resource demand. These works directly address multi-objective selection in the forecasting domain, which contradicts the implication that this is the first such approach. The related work should acknowledge these existing multi-objective methods and clarify how the current approach differs beyond just considering ensembles.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim about lack of comprehensive evaluations
comments.quote: In domains such as computer vision or neural architecture search (NAS), offline computations have been harvested and leveraged successfully to perform extensive model comparisons or compare different NAS strategies *(Ying et al., 2019; Dong and Yang, 2020; Pfisterer et al., 2021)*. However, to the best of our knowledge, no comprehensive set of model evaluations has been released in the realm of forecasting.
comments.explanation: The claim that no comprehensive set of model evaluations exists in forecasting is inaccurate. The M4 and M5 forecasting competitions (Makridakis et al.) provided extensive benchmark evaluations across many models and datasets. Additionally, the GluonTS toolkit (Alexandrov et al., 2020) includes comprehensive benchmark results for multiple forecasting models. While these may not be exactly the same as NAS-style offline computations, they represent substantial comprehensive evaluations in the forecasting domain that should be acknowledged.

comments.title: Insufficient dataset heterogeneity justification
comments.quote: Our benchmark provides 44 heterogeneous public datasets in total. The datasets greatly differ in the number of time series (from 8 to $\approx$170,000), their mean length (from $\approx$22 to $\approx$500,000), their frequency (minutely, hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, yearly), and the forecast horizon (from 6 to 60). Thus, we expect them to cover a wide range of datasets encountered in practice.
comments.explanation: The claim that 44 datasets cover "a wide range of datasets encountered in practice" is not sufficiently justified. While the ranges of basic statistics are provided, there is no analysis of whether these ranges adequately represent real-world forecasting scenarios. For example, the minimum of 8 time series and mean length of 22 observations may not reflect typical business forecasting needs. The authors should provide evidence or citations showing that these parameter ranges are representative of practical applications, or acknowledge the limitations of their benchmark coverage.

comments.title: Missing dataset selection criteria
comments.quote: Datasets are taken from various forecasting competitions, the UCI *(Dua and Graff, 2017)*, and the Monash time series forecasting repository *(Godahewa et al., 2021)*. Dataset sources, descriptions, basic statistics, and an explanation of the data preparation procedure can be found in Appendix B.
comments.explanation: The section does not explain the criteria used to select these specific 44 datasets from the available repositories. Without knowing the selection methodology, readers cannot assess potential selection bias or whether the datasets were chosen to favor certain types of models or forecasting scenarios. The authors should add a brief description of their dataset selection criteria, such as ensuring coverage of different domains, data characteristics, or avoiding redundancy.

comments.title: Ambiguous model training duration specification
comments.quote: Deep learning models are trained for a fixed duration depending on the size of the dataset.
comments.explanation: The statement about training duration is too vague to ensure reproducibility. 'Fixed duration depending on dataset size' could mean many different training schedules (e.g., fixed epochs, fixed wall-clock time, early stopping criteria). Without specifying the exact relationship between dataset size and training duration, readers cannot replicate the experimental setup or understand whether all models received comparable training resources.

comments.title: Inconsistent ensemble size specification
comments.quote: Table 1 also indicates the performance of ensembles obtained by averaging the predictions of  $n = 9$  ( $n = 3$  for MQ-RNN) deep learning models trained with different hyperparameters (hyper-ensembles)
comments.explanation: The text specifies different ensemble sizes for different models (9 for most, 3 for MQ-RNN) without justification. This creates an unfair comparison since ensemble size directly impacts both accuracy and latency. The paper should either use consistent ensemble sizes across all methods or provide a clear rationale for the different sizes. If MQ-RNN requires smaller ensembles due to computational constraints, this should be explicitly stated and discussed as a limitation. Otherwise, the comparison becomes methodologically inconsistent.

comments.title: Mismatched parentheses in hypervolume error definition
comments.quote: $\varepsilon(\mathcal{S})=\mathcal{H}\left(\{\bm{f}(x)\ |\ x\in\mathcal{P}_{\bm{f}}\left(\mathcal{X}\right)\}\right)-\mathcal{H}\left(\{\bm{f}(x_{1}),\dots,\bm{f}(x_{n}))\}\right)\geq 0$
comments.explanation: The hypervolume error definition contains mismatched parentheses. The second hypervolume term has an extra closing parenthesis after $x_{n}$ and is missing an opening parenthesis before the set. Rewrite $\varepsilon(\mathcal{S})=\mathcal{H}\left(\{\bm{f}(x)\ |\ x\in\mathcal{P}_{\bm{f}}\left(\mathcal{X}\right)\}\right)-\mathcal{H}\left(\{\bm{f}(x_{1}),\dots,\bm{f}(x_{n}))\}\right)\geq 0$ as $\varepsilon(\mathcal{S})=\mathcal{H}\left(\{\bm{f}(x)\ |\ x\in\mathcal{P}_{\bm{f}}\left(\mathcal{X}\right)\}\right)-\mathcal{H}\left(\{\bm{f}(x_{1}),\dots,\bm{f}(x_{n})\}\right)\geq 0$ because the original expression is syntactically incorrect.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for objective function
comments.quote: where $k$ denotes a single fixed objective (for instance, the classification error).
comments.explanation: The notation $f_{k}^{(j)}(x_{i})$ is used without proper definition. The superscript $(j)$ appears to index datasets, but this conflicts with the earlier statement that $k$ denotes a single fixed objective. The function should be consistently notated as either $f_{k}^{(j)}(x_{i})$ if $(j)$ indexes datasets, or $f_{k}(x_{i}, D_j)$ if $D_j$ represents dataset $j$. The current notation creates ambiguity about whether $(j)$ is an exponent, an index, or part of the function name. Add a clear definition: "where $f_{k}^{(j)}(x_{i})$ denotes the performance of model $x_{i}$ on objective $k$ for dataset $j$" to resolve this ambiguity.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated NP-completeness claim
comments.quote: Since the problem is NP-complete, a greedy heuristic with a provable approximation guarantee is used:
comments.explanation: The claim that the problem is NP-complete is stated without justification or reference. For a selection problem with a fixed number of models $n$ and $T$ datasets, the search space is $|\mathcal{X}|^n$, which is exponential in $n$ but polynomial in $T$. The NP-completeness of this specific formulation is not obvious and requires proof or citation to established results. Either provide a reference to a known NP-complete problem that this reduces to, or remove the NP-completeness claim and simply state that the exponential search space motivates the use of heuristics.

comments.title: Contradictory performance comparison
comments.quote: For  $c \geq 100$  ms, the constrained ensemble recovers the Pareto front and outperforms all individual models  $x \in \mathcal{X}$  as well as ensembles.
comments.explanation: This sentence contains a logical contradiction. If the constrained ensemble 'outperforms all individual models $x \in \mathcal{X}$ as well as ensembles', it cannot simultaneously be part of the set of ensembles it outperforms. The authors likely meant to say it outperforms all individual models and other ensemble selection methods, but as written, the claim is incoherent. Rewrite this to clarify what baseline ensembles are being compared against.

comments.title: Overstated benchmark comprehensiveness claim
comments.quote: This constitutes, by far, the most comprehensive publicly available evaluation of forecasting methods.
comments.explanation: The claim of being "by far the most comprehensive" is an overstatement that ignores existing large-scale forecasting benchmarks. The M4/M5 competitions evaluated dozens of methods on 100,000+ time series, and the Monash Time Series Forecasting Repository (Godahewa et al., 2021) contains evaluations across 30+ datasets. While the paper's 44-dataset, 13-method evaluation is substantial, it's not clearly "by far" the most comprehensive available. The authors should temper this claim to "one of the most comprehensive" or provide specific evidence for why their benchmark surpasses existing ones in scope or quality.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of ensembling comparison practices
comments.quote: While some recent methods incorporate ensembling *(Oreshkin et al., 2019; Jeon and Seong, 2021)*, comparisons are often made against individual models which may cloud the benefit of the method proposed versus the sole benefit of ensembling.
comments.explanation: This statement mischaracterizes the evaluation practices in recent forecasting literature. Many papers that propose ensembling methods, including N-BEATS (Oreshkin et al., 2019) which is cited, do compare against individual models and also against other ensembling baselines. For example, the M4 competition winner (Makridakis et al., 2018) and subsequent ensemble methods typically provide comparisons against both individual models and other ensemble approaches. The claim that comparisons "often" ignore the ensembling benefit seems overstated without specific evidence.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces a benchmark for time series forecasting models and a method, ParetoSelect, to identify Pareto-optimal model configurations under multi-objective constraints like accuracy and latency. While the benchmark is useful, the methodology for learning default models relies on assumptions about surrogate model performance that require more rigorous empirical validation.

The paper provides a valuable contribution by releasing a large-scale evaluation of forecasting models across diverse datasets. The proposed ParetoSelect approach effectively addresses the practical need for balancing accuracy with resource constraints. However, the reliance on a specific surrogate architecture and the potential for overfitting the selection strategy to the benchmark datasets need more careful investigation.

Overview issues:

- Sensitivity of Surrogate Performance: Section 4.2 describes a relatively simple MLP surrogate with fixed hyperparameters to predict model performance. The paper does not demonstrate how sensitive the ParetoSelect recommendations are to the surrogate's architecture or its training stability. If the surrogate fails to accurately rank models on a new, unseen dataset, the selected 'defaults' may be significantly suboptimal. A sensitivity analysis or a comparison against alternative surrogate models (e.g., Random Forests or Gaussian Processes) would strengthen the claim that this approach is generalizable.

- Assumption of Metric Independence: The methodology in Section 4.1 and 4.2 assumes that objectives like nCRPS and latency can be optimized independently or via a simple non-dominated sorting of surrogate outputs. However, in time series forecasting, accuracy and latency often exhibit non-linear or correlated behavior depending on the model's complexity and the data's characteristics. The paper should explicitly address whether the surrogate model captures these interactions, perhaps by providing a visualization of the predicted vs. actual Pareto front for a held-out dataset.

- Generalizability and Data Leakage Risks: The paper uses a leave-one-out cross-validation approach on 44 datasets to evaluate ParetoSelect. Given the significant heterogeneity of these datasets (from minutely sensor data to yearly economic series), it is unclear if the learned defaults are truly 'universal' or if they are biased toward the specific distribution of datasets in the benchmark. The paper would benefit from a more rigorous zero-shot evaluation on a completely independent set of datasets not included in the original 44 to demonstrate that the selection strategy does not overfit the benchmark.

- Temporal Non-stationarity in Model Selection: The paper treats model selection as a static task, yet time series are inherently non-stationary. The evaluation metrics and the surrogate training process do not account for how the Pareto front might shift as a time series evolves or as data quality changes over time. Section 4.4 discusses latency-constrained ensembles, but it ignores the potential need for dynamic model retraining or selection triggers. A discussion on how the selected defaults perform under distribution shift would be a significant improvement.

- Demonstration of ParetoSelect on a held-out, independent dataset: The paper relies on leave-one-out cross-validation across 44 datasets to demonstrate the effectiveness of ParetoSelect. While this shows performance within the benchmark, it does not confirm the claim that the method learns 'default models' for truly unseen applications. To provide evidence that the method generalizes, the authors should perform a zero-shot evaluation on a completely independent, external dataset not included in the original 44. This would demonstrate that the learned surrogate model is robust and not merely overfitting the specific characteristics of the benchmark repository.

- Visualization of surrogate-predicted versus actual Pareto fronts: The paper claims ParetoSelect effectively identifies models on the Pareto front, but it relies on a simple MLP surrogate to rank these models. It is unclear if the surrogate captures the true trade-off surface or if it merely approximates a subset of it. The paper should include a plot comparing the predicted Pareto front (from the surrogate) against the ground-truth Pareto front for a representative held-out dataset. This would provide necessary intuition on whether the surrogate's ranking accuracy is sufficient to guide model selection in practice.

- Implementation guidance for practitioners regarding surrogate training: The paper proposes a novel method but provides limited guidance on the practical requirements for training the surrogate model. Specifically, it does not discuss the minimum number of offline evaluations or the diversity of datasets required for the surrogate to converge to a useful ranking. Adding a brief analysis on how the hypervolume error (Eq. 7) scales with the number of training tasks $T$ would provide critical guidance for practitioners who might want to apply this method to their own, smaller collections of datasets.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a strong empirical benchmark, but the proposed ParetoSelect method requires more robust validation to ensure its recommendations are reliable across diverse, unseen scenarios. The current evaluation is too tightly coupled to the provided benchmark datasets, leaving open questions about the method's real-world robustness.

Revision targets:

- Perform a sensitivity analysis of the surrogate model architecture to demonstrate that the selection results are not artifacts of the chosen MLP configuration.

- Conduct a zero-shot evaluation on a set of external, non-benchmark datasets to test the generalizability of the learned defaults.

- Add a discussion or empirical analysis regarding the stability of the Pareto front under temporal non-stationarity.

- Provide a clearer justification for the listwise ranking loss used in the surrogate training compared to standard regression approaches.
